description: For students pivoting from one field to another. Emphasises transferable skills, deliberate reasoning for the switch, and relevant preparation.

examples.title: Switching from Teaching to Human Resources
examples.body:
3 November 2026

Dear Human Resources Manager,

I am writing to apply for the Human Resources Assistant position at your organisation. I hold a teaching qualification and spent two years as a primary school teacher, but I am now pursuing a career in human resources, and I believe my teaching background gives me unique strengths for this field.

Teaching taught me skills that transfer directly to HR. I managed classrooms of 40 students, which required conflict resolution, clear communication, and the ability to motivate diverse individuals, skills that apply equally to managing employee relations. I conducted parent-teacher conferences where I had to deliver difficult feedback diplomatically, a skill essential for performance management conversations. I also coordinated with school administrators on staffing, scheduling, and policy implementation, giving me exposure to organisational processes.

To prepare for this transition, I completed a certificate in Human Resource Management through the Zambia Institute of Human Resource Management. The programme covered recruitment and selection, labour law, training and development, and compensation management. I also shadowed an HR officer at a local NGO for two weeks, where I observed interview processes, onboarding procedures, and employee grievance handling.

I am not asking your organisation to take a risk on an untested candidate. I am offering a candidate who has proven soft skills, a genuine passion for people development, and the humility to start fresh in a new field.

I would welcome the opportunity to discuss my application.

Yours sincerely,

Prisca Lungu

examples.title: Pivot from Nursing to Health Informatics
examples.body:
15 July 2026

Dear Recruitment Team,

I am applying for the Health Informatics Intern position. I am a registered nurse with three years of clinical experience, and I am now pursuing a career in health informatics because I believe that technology is the key to improving healthcare delivery in Zambia.

My nursing career has given me firsthand insight into the information challenges facing our healthcare system. I have spent hours searching for patient records in poorly organised filing systems. I have seen treatment errors caused by illegible handwriting and missing allergy information. I have watched colleagues struggle with outdated software that was clearly designed by people who never set foot in a clinic. These experiences convinced me that the intersection of healthcare and technology is where I can make the biggest impact.

To prepare for this transition, I completed a diploma in Health Information Systems and learned database management, electronic health record design, and health data standards. I also taught myself SQL and basic Python, skills I used to automate a simple patient tracking spreadsheet for my ward, which reduced lost follow-up appointments by 30%.

I bring to this role something that pure technologists lack: deep clinical knowledge. I understand the workflows, the terminology, the urgency, and the stakes. I can translate between clinical staff and IT teams because I have been both.

I am eager to contribute to your health informatics projects and to grow into a specialist who bridges the gap between medicine and technology.

Yours sincerely,

Monica Tembo

examples.title: Transition from Agriculture to Agribusiness Finance
examples.body:
8 January 2026

Dear Sir/Madam,

I am writing to apply for the Agricultural Lending Officer position at your bank. I hold a diploma in Agricultural Science from Zambia College of Agriculture and spent four years working as an extension officer in Southern Province, but I am now transitioning into agricultural finance.

My extension work gave me deep knowledge of smallholder farming, crop cycles, input costs, and market access challenges. I worked with over 200 farmers, advising them on seed selection, fertiliser application, pest management, and post-harvest handling. I saw how access to credit, or the lack of it, determined whether a farmer could invest in improved seeds, irrigation, or storage. I also saw how poorly designed loan products, with rigid repayment schedules that ignored harvest timing, caused more harm than good.

To prepare for finance, I enrolled in a part-time Bachelor of Business Administration programme, specialising in finance. I have completed courses in financial analysis, credit risk assessment, and agricultural economics. I understand balance sheets, cash flow statements, and the key ratios used to assess borrower creditworthiness. I also understand that for farmers, cash flow is seasonal, not monthly, and that collateral is often land or crops rather than fixed assets.

I believe I can design and manage loan products that actually work for Zambian farmers, because I understand both sides, the farmer's reality and the bank's risk requirements.

I would welcome the opportunity to contribute to your agricultural lending portfolio.

Yours faithfully,

Stephen Mweetwa
